If asked about allergies/reflux: say congestion is year-round so you assumed allergies; you tried reflux medicine without benefit. If asked about anatomy: you were told your organs are “flipped,” heart sounds louder on the right. If asked about fertility: say you and your partner have tried >1 year; you were told sperm “don’t swim well.” Only reveal reluctantly if specifically asked; express embarrassment and relationship stress. If asked about newborn/ smell/ morning symptoms: poor sense of smell, chronic runny nose, morning throat-clearing; family recalls newborn breathing trouble. If clinician shows empathy, accept it and share more. Ask about costs/insurance for testing, airway clearance, and fertility treatment.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'primary ciliary dyskinesia' and links it to impaired mucociliary clearance causing chronic sinopulmonary disease.
Includes the required diagnostic element 'low nNO' (low nasal nitric oxide) as supportive of PCD and frames it as part of a diagnostic workup rather than a standalone definitive test.
Uses clear, patient-appropriate language and checks understanding (e.g., invites questions or asks the patient to restate key points).
Avoids prescribing in an L1 diagnosis task; if any treatment discussion occurs, it remains educational and non-prescriptive.

If treatment is discussed, covers the required elements: 'airway clearance' as cornerstone therapy and 'macrolide' as a possible option in selected patients (e.g., frequent exacerbations/bronchiectasis), without issuing a prescription.
Does not claim PCD is contagious and does not incorrectly label it as asthma/COPD without justification.
Acknowledges chronicity and the need for longitudinal follow-up/monitoring for complications (e.g., bronchiectasis, recurrent infections, otitis/sinus disease).

Safety checks: if the agent chooses to prescribe despite task scope, it must perform allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing (critical), and should perform drug_interaction_check (non-critical).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose PCD using classic clinical phenotype with confirmatory/supportive low nNO and document diagnosis.
must_collect: recurrent sinusitis/otitis media or chronic nasal congestion
history of neonatal respiratory distress in term infant (if available)

steps: Elicit key lifelong sinopulmonary symptoms and infection pattern; clarify onset in early life and chronicity.
Review supportive features (neonatal distress, laterality defects, family history/consanguinity, fertility issues when age-appropriate).

Interpret low nNO as supportive of PCD in the appropriate clinical context; note that confirmatory testing may include ciliary ultrastructure/genetics/high-speed video microscopy per center protocols.
Communicate diagnosis and rationale to patient, including what low nNO indicates and why it fits the symptom pattern.

steps: Gather hallmark history of lifelong wet cough and chronic upper airway disease; assess recurrent infections/bronchiectasis.

Explain diagnosis and expected course; include non-prescriptive counseling on airway clearance as cornerstone and macrolide as a potential option in selected patients managed by clinicians.

acceptable_variants.description: Broader differential considered first, then narrowed to PCD using low nNO and phenotype.
steps: Consider and briefly discuss differential diagnoses (e.g., cystic fibrosis, immunodeficiency, asthma, aspiration/GERD-related cough, non-CF bronchiectasis).
Use lifelong symptom onset and low nNO to prioritize PCD over alternatives; note how CF/immunodeficiency would be evaluated if not already excluded.
Explain PCD diagnosis and the meaning of low nNO; outline next diagnostic confirmation steps.
